title: InteractiveAuthorizationRequired error when tyring to restart system service as user
author: teto
body: when I am trying to restart a system-level service I get a `org.freedesktop.DBus.Error.InteractiveAuthorizationRequired` error. Message makes it sound like this needs systemctl-tui modifications: I am not sure wether that's the case or if it's my system that needs tweaking ?

title: Limit units not filtering
author: moussaclarke
body: ### Description Limit units no longer seems to work. This looks like a regression since version 0.5.0, working fine in 0.4.1. ### Steps to reproduce: Start systemctl-tui with a specific service or services e.g `systemctl-tui -l postgresql.service` ### Expected result: To see only the specific service(s) in the left hand list. ### Actual result: Can see all services in the left hand list. ### Screenshots: **v0.5.1** **v0.4.1 (Correct behaviour)** ### Environment - OS: Ubuntu 25.10 x86_64 - Kernel: 6.14.0-37-generic - Terminal: WezTerm

title: Feature request: Add ability to select logs and copy-paste
author: woile
body:
Hi there, thanks for this awesome tool! It's helped me familiarize and debug app using systemctl a lot.

One thing I find myself trying to do, is select logs, trying to copy to search the errors, but I cannot do that.
I searched a bit around the repo, but I couldn't find anything, apoligies if it was already discussed.

Would it be possible to allow selecting text and copying it?

Best!

author: magnetophon
body:
Thank you for fixing https://github.com/rgwood/systemctl-tui/issues/59

For the benefit of distros and packagers: please do a new release.

author: magnetophon
body: On my light theme, the top line cannot be fully read:

title: View full logs with better browsing and searching support
author: BLumia
body:
Related:

- https://github.com/rgwood/systemctl-tui/issues/52

Currently we can view the log of the selected service unit, and do line-by-line scrolling or jump to the beginning/end point of the log. This is handy, but it could be better if we can have better navigation support and find/search keyword in the log.

Any approach are appreciated. A possible approach I got in mind that might work is: Allow user switch "focus" to the log pane, and then allow using <kbd>/</kbd> to search and vim bindings/arrowkeys/pageupdown to scroll the view. If user want to search/filter service units, simply let the user switch the focus back to the service unit pane.

title: Can't scroll logs to the bottom
author: dolzenko
body: 0.4.0, arch. After pressing Down/End logs seem to be stuck at the same line. Pressing Home shows logs from 2025-10-14, but pushing End then shows 2025-10-13

title: Filter by unit status (or filter failed units)
author: chadsr
body:
It would be great to have an option/flag to filter by unit status, most importantly (for me at least) filtering by failed units.

Maybe a flag `-f` could be used to provide this functionality?

title: COSMIC desktop
author: westurner
body:
AFAIU COSMIC (a rust-based window manager and userspace) does not have a systemctl TUI or GUI.

- https://github.com/pop-os/cosmic-epoch/
- https://github.com/pop-os?q=systemd&type=all&language=&sort=
  - https://github.com/pop-os/systemd-boot-conf
    > Rust crate for convenient handling of the systemd-boot loader configuration, as well as the loader entries that it maintains. This may be used to modify the loader configuration, create new loader entries, or modify existing ones.
    - Could systemctl-tui also show the systemd-boot loader entries?
    - Could systemctl-tui also show the rpm-ostree (ostree + grub) boot loader entries?
      - https://github.com/Lifailon/lazyjournal reads logs from journald, auditd, containers, k8s
- https://github.com/cosmic-utils?q=systemd
- https://github.com/cosmic-utils/wizard

(rpm-ostree pulls /root fs images and then layers on /etc, /var/home, and /usr)

There are (bootc) native container images of Fedora Silverblue (Gnome) and Knoite (KDE) with COSMIC desktop that I've been meaning to test out with `rpm-ostree rebase` (on this Silverblue box) one of these days but I would use a VM instead of the limited number of boot options that rpm-ostree currently supports by default. 

- https://github.com/ublue-os/cosmic?tab=readme-ov-file
  - https://github.com/ublue-os/main/pkgs/container/cosmic-atomic-main

uutils/coreutils probably doesn't yet link to systemd?

- https://github.com/uutils/coreutils/

RedoxOS does not have a system service management gui; or a complete init story yet IIUC:

- RedoxOS doesn't have systemd; it's called init
  - https://github.com/redox-os/init

title: View full logs with scroll/browse support
author: theSprog
body:
### Summary

Thank you for `systemctl-tui` — it's really useful! One thing I noticed is that the built-in log viewer only shows the beginning of the Service Logs, and doesn't appear to support scrolling or full viewing.

### Use case

In many situations (e.g., debugging long-running services), I need to:

- Read logs beyond the initial few lines
- Scroll vertically through the full `journalctl` output
- Ideally follow logs (`journalctl -fu <unit>`) or search within them

### Questions

- Is there a way to configure the number of lines shown, or scroll through logs that I may have missed?
- If not, would you consider adding a scrollable pager (e.g., using `less` or an internal scroll view) to improve the log viewer?

Happy to provide more details or help test this. Thanks again!

title: Scope switching inside TUI
author: theSprog
body:
### Summary

Currently, `systemctl-tui` only supports selecting the unit scope (system vs user) via the `--scope` flag at startup. There's no apparent way to switch scopes once inside the TUI. This makes it difficult to manage both system and user units in a single session.

### What I expected

It would be very helpful if there was an interactive way (e.g., a shortcut key like `s` or `u`) to toggle between scopes at runtime. This is especially useful for users who manage both system-wide services and per-user services (e.g. using `systemd --user` sessions).

### What I observed

On version `0.4.0`, once the TUI starts, the selected scope appears fixed for the entire session, and no keybinding seems available to switch between `system` and `user` modes.

### Questions

- Is there currently any hidden way to switch scopes interactively?
- If not, would it be feasible to add this as a feature in a future release?

Thanks for the great project!

title: "Unable to get services" on Debian Bookworm/Trixie
author: zuavra
body:
Attempting to run the static `systemctl-tui` binary from the 0.4.0 release tarball, as root, on an up-to-date Debian Bookworm, fails with the following error:

```
Error: Unable to get services. Check that systemd is running and try running this tool with sudo.

Caused by:
    org.freedesktop.DBus.Error.ServiceUnknown: The name org.freedesktop.systemd1 was not provided by any .service files
```

This is a very basic Debian install used mostly for running Docker containers.

```
Linux nas 6.1.0-37-amd64 #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Debian 6.1.140-1 (2025-05-22) x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

Output of `busctl`:

```
NAME                      PID PROCESS USER CONNECTION    UNIT SESSION DESCRIPTION
:1.0                        9 n/a     root :1.0          -    -       -          
:1.1                        9 n/a     root :1.1          -    -       -          
:1.31                       - -       -    -             -    -       -          
com.hp.hplip                - -       -    (activatable) -    -       -          
org.freedesktop.Avahi       - -       -    (activatable) -    -       -          
org.freedesktop.DBus  1941947 n/a     root -             -    -       -          
```

Output of `dpkg -l | grep -i dbus`:

```
ii  dbus                            1.14.10-1~deb12u1                       amd64        simple interprocess messaging system (system message bus)
ii  dbus-bin                        1.14.10-1~deb12u1                       amd64        simple interprocess messaging system (command line utilities)
ii  dbus-daemon                     1.14.10-1~deb12u1                       amd64        simple interprocess messaging system (reference message bus)
ii  dbus-session-bus-common         1.14.10-1~deb12u1                       all          simple interprocess messaging system (session bus configuration)
ii  dbus-system-bus-common          1.14.10-1~deb12u1                       all          simple interprocess messaging system (system bus configuration)
ii  dbus-user-session               1.14.10-1~deb12u1                       amd64        simple interprocess messaging system (systemd --user integration)
ii  libdbus-1-3:amd64               1.14.10-1~deb12u1                       amd64        simple interprocess messaging system (library)
```

Output of `dpkg -l | grep -i systemd`:

```
ii  dbus-user-session               1.14.10-1~deb12u1                       amd64        simple interprocess messaging system (systemd --user integration)
ii  libnss-systemd:amd64            252.38-1~deb12u1                        amd64        nss module providing dynamic user and group name resolution
ii  libpam-systemd:amd64            252.38-1~deb12u1                        amd64        system and service manager - PAM module
ii  libsystemd-shared:amd64         252.38-1~deb12u1                        amd64        systemd shared private library
ii  libsystemd0:amd64               252.38-1~deb12u1                        amd64        systemd utility library
ii  systemd                         252.38-1~deb12u1                        amd64        system and service manager
ii  systemd-sysv                    252.38-1~deb12u1                        amd64        system and service manager - SysV compatibility symlinks
ii  systemd-timesyncd               252.38-1~deb12u1                        amd64        minimalistic service to synchronize local time with NTP servers
ii  systemd-zram-generator          1.1.2-2+b2                              amd64        Systemd unit generator for zram devices
```

Output of `systemctl status dbus-org.freedesktop.systemd1.service`:

```
Unit dbus-org.freedesktop.systemd1.service could not be found.
```

Output of `systemctl status dbus-org.freedesktop.[TAB][TAB]`:

```
dbus-org.freedesktop.hostname1.service
dbus-org.freedesktop.login1.service
dbus-org.freedesktop.timesync1.service
dbus-org.freedesktop.locale1.service
dbus-org.freedesktop.timedate1.service
```

`/usr/share/dbus-1/services/org.freedesktop.systemd1.service` exists and is a symlink that points to `/usr/share/dbus-1/system-services/org.freedesktop.systemd1.service` which belongs to `systemd` package and contains:

```
#  SPDX-License-Identifier: LGPL-2.1-or-later
#
#  This file is part of systemd.
#
#  systemd is free software; you can redistribute it and/or modify it
#  under the terms of the GNU Lesser General Public License as published by
#  the Free Software Foundation; either version 2.1 of the License, or
#  (at your option) any later version.

[D-BUS Service]
Name=org.freedesktop.systemd1
Exec=/bin/false
User=root
```

Please let me know if there's any other information I can add.

title: support for remote connections
author: dustymabe
body:
A killer feature here would be being able to browse remote systems (i.e. via SSH). This way it wouldn't require installing `systemctl-tui` on the remote systems themselves, but just initiating the connections from your desktop/laptop and rendering the results (from the remote machine) back in the TUI window on the local machine. I know `systemctl` has `--host` for something like this.

I think the blocker may be journalctl since it doesn't have support for `--host` yet: https://github.com/systemd/systemd/issues/4793

title: feat request: support create a new service by some prompts
author: rust-kotlin
body:
Perhaps We could use this library to help create a new service by sequently asking

1. Name
2. Description(could be null)
3. Working Directory(could be null)
4. Exec Command

Then we could add some custom modifications to the file generated file by tempelate.

Indeed I have already finished this function roughly. If you think it's worth doing so, I would be glad to make more improvements and create a pull request.

This is the code compare of my commits: https://github.com/rgwood/systemctl-tui/compare/master...rust-kotlin:systemctl-tui:master

title: Feature request: `systemctl kill` support
author: brainwo
body:
`systemctl` allows you to send kill signal to a running service process using `systemctl kill`. There are a lot of kill signals that are useful, some are demonstrated in this related issue #38.

My personal use case is to send a user defined signal `SIGUSR1`:
```
systemctl --user kill --signal=SIGUSR1 process
```
`SIGUSR1` and `SIGUSR2` are used by some programs to essentially communicate with the program for a specific purpose.

My idea is to be able to choose which signal to send, like how `htop` allow you to send kill signal when killing a process:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d2a392da-3eb3-481a-909b-0c39f7f8382f)

This would be a great addition to `systemctl-tui` since typing it manually is very unintuitive with many services to kill and many signals to choose.

---

Thank you for the amazing work. `systemctl-tui` has really helped me manage my systemd services.

title: Optional logging
author: muchgitsuchwow
body:
Hey there,

from looking at utils.rs there seems to be no way to disable writing log files somewhere to ~/.local/share/systemctl-tui. I'd love to contribute to make this an optional excersize, but me writing Rust is probably never going to happen. So I ask here if anyone is capable to do that if it would be an acceptable feature.

Thanks (for this piece of software)!

title: alternative to select
author: yara-blue
body:
Hi there, lovely TUI. I was browsing through the code to steal your systemd interface :) for my installer builder crate (which now calls systemctl and parses its output).

I noticed this comment:
https://github.com/rgwood/systemctl-tui/blob/feb0c475815f53c04baaa29f1a9686e0d447bead/src/systemd.rs#L217

I wonder if [futures_concurrency](https://docs.rs/futures-concurrency/latest/futures_concurrency/index.html) might offer a good fit? I really like it myself.

Thats all.

Have a great day!

Edit:
`select` is called `race` in *futures_concurrency*

title: Allow using sudo when needed
author: getzze
body:
Thanks for the great tool, it's very appreciated!

Currently (v0.4.0), when running `systemctl-tui`, I can see all the system services but when I want to start one, I get an error, Permission denied, of some sort. So I need to run it with `sudo systemctl-tui`. However I don't see my user services with `sudo systemctl-tui`.

I would like to be able to run `systemctl-tui` unprivileged, and be ask of the sudo password when I need to, to start/stop a service for instance.
[paru](https://github.com/Morganamilo/paru) does that for instance.

title: Support freeze / thaw services
author: sanchezcarlosjr
body:
Thank you for the awesome project, it brings a lot to the community.

# Why?
Similarly as `kill` handles the signals `CONT` and `STOP` with traditional process management, `systemd` supports freezing and thawing services. This allows suspending a service, and afterward resuming it. Contrary to the `kill` program, `systemd` suspend the whole process tree and offers more fine control. So, this feature is really handy and brings more usefulness to the `systemctl-tui` project.

# Description
Add `systemctl freeze --unit X` and `systemctl thaw --unit X` as freeze and thaw options in the Actions menu.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/be8fc853-3eff-4277-973b-c3cd432419a7)
